title: STEMI Recognition in the Field: Prehospital ECG Essentials for Paramedics
excerpt: Build a disciplined approach to prehospital 12-lead acquisition, STEMI-equivalent patterns, mimics, and activation documentation that holds up on exams and in QA.
focus: acute coronary occlusion patterns, reciprocal change, and time-sensitive cath lab activation
scenario: You are dispatched for chest pressure in a diaphoretic patient with nausea. Vitals show sinus tachycardia and hypertension; SpO2 is 94% on room air. The patient reports crushing substernal pain radiating to the left arm for 35 minutes.
examAngle: For ACS items, prioritize high-quality ECG early, oxygen when hypoxemic per your course, aspirin when protocol allows, and rapid transport with notification when STEMI criteria are met.

title: FAST and BEFAST Stroke Screening for EMS: Field Guide and Pitfalls
excerpt: Translate stroke screening tools into a consistent EMS workflow that protects time windows, catches mimics, and documents the data stroke teams need.
focus: lateralizing deficits, sudden onset, last known well, and glucose verification before thrombolysis conversations
scenario: A family member reports slurred speech and right-sided weakness starting 40 minutes ago. The patient is anxious but able to stand with assistance. Blood glucose is 118 mg/dL.
examAngle: Registry-style questions reward last known well documentation, sudden focal neuro change, early notification, and avoiding false reassurance when symptoms fluctuate.

title: Glasgow Coma Scale in EMS: Practical Scoring, Pitfalls, and Documentation
excerpt: Score GCS reliably in noisy environments, understand what each domain measures, and connect GCS trends to airway decisions and trauma triage.
focus: eye, verbal, and motor components with intoxication, language barriers, and pediatric caveats per training
scenario: An intoxicated patient opens eyes to voice, uses inappropriate words, and localizes pain. Ten minutes later after naloxone, the patient is oriented but somnolent.
examAngle: Exams test reassessment after intervention and when lowered GCS mandates airway vigilance even if numbers improve partially.

title: Pediatric Respiratory Distress: Prehospital Assessment and Escalation for EMS
excerpt: Use work of breathing, mental status, and perfusion to stratify pediatric respiratory emergencies and choose interventions aligned with protocol.
focus: stridor, wheeze, crackles, retractions, and fatigue patterns that predict imminent failure
scenario: A 3-year-old with a barky cough has mild stridor at rest and moderate retractions. The child is alert but tiring, with tachycardia and tachypnea.
examAngle: Pediatric items often reward identifying fatigue and altered mentation as escalation triggers beyond oxygen saturation alone.

title: Shock Types for Paramedics: Field Recognition, Clues, and Treatment Priorities
excerpt: Differentiate hypovolemic, obstructive, cardiogenic, and distributive shock using bedside patterns and connect each pattern to protocol branches.
focus: pulse pressure, skin, JVD clues when appropriate, lung exam, and hemorrhage control as first-line therapy when indicated
scenario: A trauma patient is cool and clammy with weak pulses and a narrowed pulse pressure after controlled external bleeding. Another patient is warm and flushed with bounding pulses early in sepsis.
examAngle: Questions pair mechanism with shock category; choose fluids, vasopressors, or procedures by category and stability.

title: SVT vs VT on the Monitor: Narrow and Wide Complex Tachycardia for EMS
excerpt: Stabilize unstable tachycardia first, then use regularity, QRS width, and clinical context to choose synchronized cardioversion versus pharmacologic pathways per protocol.
focus: unstable versus stable branches, aberrancy suspicion, and post-conversion reassessment
scenario: A patient with palpitations has a regular wide complex rhythm at 180 with hypotension and altered mental status.
examAngle: Unstable wide complex tachycardia commonly leads to immediate synchronized cardioversion when hypotension and altered mentation are present.

title: Prehospital Oxygen Therapy: Targets, Risks, and Protocol-Aligned EMS Practice
excerpt: Apply oxygen titration thinking to COPD, ACS, and post-ROSC patients while documenting indications and reassessment after every change.
focus: avoiding hyperoxia harm contexts taught in your program while preventing hypoxia during acute illness
scenario: A COPD patient is in distress with SpO2 82% on room air but improves to 88% on low-flow oxygen with reduced work of breathing.
examAngle: Expect questions that reward titration to protocol targets rather than reflexive high-flow oxygen in every dyspnea patient.

title: Sepsis Recognition in EMS: Vital Signs, Lactate, and Early Bundle Thinking
excerpt: Recognize sepsis early using EMS screening patterns, understand what lactate adds when available, and document infection suspicion plus perfusion cues.
focus: tachypnea, altered mentation, hypotension, and source clues with timely transport and notification
scenario: An older adult with suspected urinary source has fever, tachypnea, and mild confusion with borderline hypotension for their baseline.
examAngle: Exams reward aggressive escalation for suspected sepsis with shock while staying inside protocol for fluids and antibiotics timing as taught.

title: Trauma Triage Basics for EMS: START, JumpSTART, and Transport Decisions
excerpt: Review triage sorting in multi-patient incidents, pediatric modifications, and how triage category influences transport order and destination.
focus: RPM-style sorting, resource allocation, and re-triage when patient condition changes
scenario: Multiple patients exit a bus rollover. You are first unit on scene and must establish command and initial triage tags.
examAngle: MCI questions reward command structure, safety, and rapid sorting over deep treatment in the hot zone.

title: Spinal Motion Restriction Updates: What EMS Students Should Know in 2026
excerpt: Understand selective spinal motion restriction concepts, patient-centered rationale, and how exam items align with contemporary position statements.
focus: mechanism risk, neurologic exam documentation, and packaging that balances immobilization harm versus benefit
scenario: A low-mechanism fall with midline tenderness and intoxication complicates decision-making about spinal precautions.
examAngle: Expect nuance: selective criteria versus historical automatic long-boarding, aligned to your textbook and agency policy.

title: Anaphylaxis in the Field: Epinephrine Timing, Routes, and EMS Protocols
excerpt: Treat anaphylaxis as airway and circulation emergency, prioritize epinephrine per protocol, and document refractory course and repeat dosing rules.
focus: urticaria versus airway involvement, hypotension, stridor, and biphasic risk education
scenario: After bee sting, the patient develops widespread hives, hoarseness, and hypotension within minutes.
examAngle: Exam keys typically prioritize IM epinephrine early for anaphylaxis rather than antihistamines alone when systemic criteria are met.

title: CPAP for Acute Pulmonary Edema: Prehospital Indications, Monitoring, and Pitfalls
excerpt: Use CPAP as a ventilation and work-of-breathing intervention while monitoring blood pressure, mental status, and gastric distention risks.
focus: respiratory distress with alveolar flooding pattern, contraindications taught in your course, and transport readiness
scenario: A patient in flash pulmonary edema is sitting upright, tripoding, with pink frothy sputum and SpO2 84%.
examAngle: Questions often connect CPAP with nitrate therapy only when hypotension is absent and protocol permits.

title: Naloxone (Narcan) in EMS: Dosing Strategies, Re-sedation, and Harm Reduction Context
excerpt: Reverse opioid toxicity while protecting airway, titrating to breathing when protocol allows, and documenting re-sedation risk.
focus: respiratory rate, pupils as supportive not definitive, and polysubstance considerations
scenario: An unconscious patient with respiratory depression receives naloxone and awakens aggressively with vomiting risk.
examAngle: Airway protection and transport after reversal commonly outrank leaving the patient on scene once reversed.

title: Prehospital Airway Adjuncts: OPA, NPA, SGA, and When Each Helps or Harms
excerpt: Choose adjuncts based on gag reflex, trauma considerations, and ventilation plan, then confirm with lung sounds and capnography when available.
focus: adjuncts as bridges to definitive airway and as BVM quality multipliers
scenario: A semi-conscious overdose patient cannot tolerate an OPA but needs assisted ventilation during transport.
examAngle: Contraindications to NPA in facial trauma or suspected basal skull injury appear frequently as safety traps.

title: EtCO2 Waveform Interpretation for EMS: Airway, Ventilation, and Circulation Clues
excerpt: Read waveform shape and numeric trends for tube confirmation, ROSC detection, bronchospasm patterns, and perfusion changes during resuscitation.
focus: quantitative versus qualitative devices, esophageal intubation absence pattern, and hyperventilation effects
scenario: During CPR, EtCO2 is flatline; after a pause and pulse check, you see a sudden rise in EtCO2 with a palpable pulse.
examAngle: ROSC recognition via EtCO2 is a classic high-yield concept alongside continuous waveform for tube surveillance.

title: Hypoglycemia Emergencies in EMS: Recognition, Dextrose, Glucagon, and Transport Rules
excerpt: Treat low glucose as altered mental status driver, document pre- and post-treatment neuro exams, and handle recurrent hypoglycemia per protocol.
focus: seizure, stroke mimic, and post-treatment observation for recurrent hypoglycemia
scenario: A diabetic patient is confused, diaphoretic, and combative with a glucose of 42 mg/dL.
examAngle: Exams reward checking glucose early in altered mental status and reassessing after treatment.

title: Burn Assessment Basics for EMS: TBSA Estimation, Inhalation Risk, and Transport
excerpt: Estimate burn size using rule of nines and palm method, identify airway burn risk cues, and stabilize while avoiding harmful field cooling errors.
focus: circumferential burns, inhalation injury suspicion, and pain management documentation
scenario: A patient trapped briefly in a closed-space fire has singed nasal hairs, sooty sputum, and partial-thickness burns to the hands.
examAngle: Inhalation injury suspicion often drives early airway planning even when voice sounds okay initially.

title: Prehospital 12-Lead ECG Interpretation: STEMI Mimics and Acquisition Quality
excerpt: Reduce false activation by understanding early repolarization, LVH strain, paced rhythms, and pericarditis patterns while improving lead acquisition habits.
focus: lead placement errors, artifact, and reciprocal change as internal consistency checks
scenario: A patient with LVH has ST changes that could be strain versus acute ischemia; serial ECGs and clinical correlation matter.
examAngle: When uncertain, document suspicion, acquire serial ECGs when permitted, and communicate uncertainty to STEMI systems.

title: Pediatric Dose Calculations in EMS: Broselow, Milligram-per-Kilogram Discipline, and Checks
excerpt: Standardize pediatric dosing safety with cross-checks, length-based resuscitation tapes, and communication read-backs during high-risk medication prep.
focus: concentration errors, two-provider checks, and documenting weight method
scenario: You must prepare epinephrine for a pediatric anaphylaxis patient during noisy extrication from a vehicle.
examAngle: Medication safety and double-check culture are frequent correct answers in pediatric EMS items.

title: MCI Triage for EMS: START, JumpSTART, SALT Concepts, and Transport Priorities
excerpt: Organize mass casualty scenes with command, triage, treatment areas, and transport units while maintaining re-triage discipline.
focus: command structure, sorting accuracy, pediatric modifications, and re-triage when patient conditions change
scenario: Twenty patients are scattered across a platform after a train derailment; you must assign priorities with limited crews.
examAngle: Expect questions on immediate versus delayed categories and expectant classification in severe resource scarcity.

title: Tension Pneumothorax: Field Recognition and Needle Decompression Indications for EMS
excerpt: Connect clinical tension physiology to protocol indications, post-decompression monitoring, and transport priorities.
focus: unilateral breath sounds, hypotension, distended neck veins when not hypovolemic, and obstructive shock pattern
scenario: A ventilated trauma patient suddenly drops saturation and blood pressure with high peak pressures.
examAngle: Obstructive shock with suspected tension often prioritizes decompression when exam and device data align with protocol.

title: Status Epilepticus in EMS: Benzodiazepine Strategies, Airway Timing, and Transport
excerpt: Stop prolonged seizure activity per protocol, protect airway, monitor respirations after benzodiazepines, and document timing for ED continuation.
focus: refractory pathways as hospital continuation, not field improvisation beyond protocol
scenario: A generalized seizure has lasted eight minutes with two benzodiazepine doses and continued convulsive activity.
examAngle: Airway support after sedation and rapid transport commonly outrank repeated blind dosing without reassessment.

title: Heat Stroke vs Heat Exhaustion: Field Differentiation and Cooling Strategies for EMS
excerpt: Identify central nervous system dysfunction as heat stroke, initiate rapid cooling per protocol, and avoid mistaking exertional collapse for benign fatigue.
focus: core temperature measurement when available, shivering management, and cardiac monitoring during cooling
scenario: A football athlete collapses confused with hot dry skin after practice on a humid day.
examAngle: CNS dysfunction plus hyperthermia after heat exposure typically drives aggressive cooling and rapid transport.

title: Drowning and Submersion Injury: Prehospital Resuscitation Priorities for EMS
excerpt: Prioritize oxygenation, ventilation, hypothermia considerations, and cervical spine precautions only when mechanism supports them.
focus: aspiration physiology, gastric contents risk, and post-return of circulation monitoring
scenario: A child is pulled from a pool cyanotic and apneic; bystander CPR is ongoing when you arrive.
examAngle: High-quality CPR and ventilation correction are central; match spine motion decisions to mechanism per training.

title: Suspected Pulmonary Embolism in EMS: Prehospital Recognition Without Overconfidence
excerpt: Recognize PE risk patterns, support oxygenation and circulation, avoid false reassurance from normal SpO2 alone, and document suspicion cleanly.
focus: tachypnea, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, syncope, and risk factors such as recent immobility when appropriate to ask
scenario: A postoperative traveler presents with sudden pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and mild hypoxia without fever.
examAngle: EMS exams reward stabilizing oxygenation and circulation while expediting transport rather than diagnosing definitively in the field.

title: Aortic Dissection Suspicion in the Field: EMS Assessment, Pain Patterns, and Transport
excerpt: Link tearing pain, pulse deficits, neurologic symptoms, and widened mediastinum concepts to high-risk transport and rate control per protocol.
focus: differentiating ACS, PE, and dissection by risk features without anchoring prematurely
scenario: A hypertensive patient describes sudden maximal tearing intrascapular pain with unequal radial pulses.
examAngle: Suspicion plus rapid transport and early notification beats prolonged field diagnostics when vascular catastrophe is plausible.

title: Hypertensive Emergency vs Urgency: EMS Field Clues and Cautious Blood Pressure Management
excerpt: Define end-organ threat patterns taught in your course, avoid reflexive aggressive drops in stroke contexts unless protocol directs, and document symptoms tied to organs.
focus: neurologic deficits, chest pain, pulmonary edema, renal failure symptoms, and pregnancy-related hypertension when relevant
scenario: BP is 220/130 with headache and blurred vision; neuro exam shows no focal deficits but patient is vomiting.
examAngle: Exams punish indiscriminate aggressive drops in unclear neurologic presentations; prefer protocol-guided cautions.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage Recognition for EMS: Field Stabilization and Hospital Handoff
excerpt: Quantify bleeding when possible, support perfusion, use fundal massage per protocol training, and communicate obstetric history clearly.
focus: tone, tissue, trauma, thrombin frameworks as memory aids without replacing hospital care
scenario: A home birth patient is pale with a boggy uterus and ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding minutes after delivery.
examAngle: Massive hemorrhage triggers large-bore access, hemorrhage pathways per protocol, and urgent transport.

title: Neonatal Resuscitation Basics for EMS: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, and Thermal Care
excerpt: Prepare for rare field deliveries with warming, positioning, suction indications, ventilation corrective steps, and CPR ratios per current training guidelines you are tested on.
focus: heart rate response to ventilation as the primary feedback loop in neonatal resuscitation teaching
scenario: A precipitous delivery occurs in an ambulance; the newborn is floppy with a slow heart rate after initial drying.
examAngle: Ventilation optimization before compressions is a frequent teaching point—match your textbook algorithm version.

title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning in EMS: The SpO2 Trap, Symptoms, and High-Flow Oxygen
excerpt: Explain why pulse oximetry can mislead, identify exposure histories, and prioritize high-flow oxygen and transport for definitive care.
focus: multi-patient exposure scenes, pregnancy risk, and delayed neuro sequelae education
scenario: Multiple family members have headache and nausea in winter with a faulty furnace history; SpO2 reads normal.
examAngle: History plus symptoms with oxygen and transport beats relying on pulse oximetry alone.

title: Organophosphate Exposure: SLUDGE Signs, EMS Decontamination, and Atropine Thinking
excerpt: Recognize cholinergic toxidrome patterns, prioritize provider safety with decon, and align airway management with secretion burden.
focus: bradycardia, bronchorrhea, pinpoint pupils, and muscle fasciculations as a cluster pattern
scenario: An agricultural worker collapses near a sprayer with pinpoint pupils, excessive secretions, and wheezing.
examAngle: Scene safety and decon precede aggressive airway management when contamination risk remains high.

title: Calcium Channel Blocker and Beta Blocker Overdose: Brady and Wide Complex Clues for EMS
excerpt: Support perfusion, recognize high-risk ECG patterns taught in toxicology modules, and prepare for hospital antidotes while managing airway and glucose.
focus: hyperglycemia patterns in BB overdose teaching pearls versus CCB patterns per coursework
scenario: An overdose patient is hypotensive with bradycardia and a wide QRS after unknown pill ingestion.
examAngle: Glucose and cardiac monitoring plus transport for advanced antidotes often beat watchful waiting when unstable.

title: Tricyclic Antidepressant Overdose in EMS: Sodium Bicarbonate Indications and Stabilization
excerpt: Link sodium channel blockade patterns to QRS widening, seizure risk, and antidote teaching while supporting airway and circulation.
focus: wide QRS, anticholinergic signs, and instability as escalation triggers per protocol education
scenario: A teenager seized after overdose; ECG shows wide QRS tachycardia with hypotension.
examAngle: Recognition and supportive care priorities remain central even when definitive antidote therapy is hospital-based.

title: Excited Delirium Presentations: EMS Safety, Restraint Ethics, and Medical Priorities (Educational)
excerpt: Study agitated delirium as a medical emergency with coordinated response, positional asphyxia avoidance, and rapid transport for reversible causes.
focus: hyperthermia, profound agitation, unexpected strength, and need for calm coordinated restraint with continuous monitoring
scenario: A patient is naked in winter, sweating, combative, and febrile with delirious speech after stimulant use is suspected.
examAngle: Safety, monitoring, and medical causes such as hypoglycemia, head injury, and hypoxia are frequent exam themes.

title: Acute Psychosis and Agitation: Prehospital Sedation Protocol Concepts for EMS
excerpt: Compare benzodiazepines versus antipsychotic approaches as taught in your protocols, emphasizing airway monitoring after sedation.
focus: verbal de-escalation first when feasible, then medication routes aligned with training
scenario: A patient in severe psychosis is attempting to assault providers and cannot be verbally redirected.
examAngle: Documentation of indication for sedation and continuous monitoring is a common correct answer theme.

title: Prehospital Pain Management: Opioids, Ketamine, and Safety Monitoring for EMS
excerpt: Titrate analgesia to effect, monitor respiratory drive, and document pain scores and adverse effects for QA and exam scenarios.
focus: trauma analgesia, dissociative dosing concepts as taught, and contraindications your protocol emphasizes
scenario: A femur fracture patient rates pain 10/10 after splinting; vitals are stable but nausea is worsening.
examAngle: Balanced analgesia with antiemetic and monitoring beats withholding treatment solely because opioids carry risk when protocol supports use.

title: ROSC After Cardiac Arrest: Prehospital Blood Pressure, Oxygenation, and Transport Priorities
excerpt: Stabilize perfusion after return of spontaneous circulation, avoid hyperventilation, titrate oxygen per post-arrest teaching, and prepare for post-arrest rhythm surveillance.
focus: 12-lead acquisition after ROSC, waveform capnography trends, and frequent reassessment
scenario: ROSC achieved after VF; patient is comatose with spontaneous respirations and fluctuating blood pressure.
examAngle: Post-ROSC care questions reward avoiding hyperventilation and aligning oxygen titration to current resuscitation education.

title: VF and Pulseless VT: Defibrillation Steps, Energy Escalation, and CPR Integration for EMS
excerpt: Practice the shock-CPR rhythm, pad placement, safety checks, and team roles for minimal pause defibrillation.
focus: biphasic energy concepts as taught, immediate CPR after shock, and reversible causes memory aids
scenario: The monitor shows VF after a witnessed collapse; AED advises shock.
examAngle: Early defibrillation and high-quality CPR are consistently prioritized over medication administration in shockable rhythms.

title: Symptomatic Bradycardia: Atropine, Pacing, and EMS Protocol Branches
excerpt: Identify unstable bradycardia, choose chronotropic therapy versus pacing per algorithm, and document transcutaneous pacing capture and patient comfort interventions.
focus: inferior MI-associated brady, high-degree block suspicion, and medication contraindications
scenario: Inferior STEMI patient has a heart rate of 38 with hypotension and altered mental status.
examAngle: Unstable bradycardia with ACS context often leads to pacing readiness and transport priority.

title: Stroke Mimics for EMS: Hypoglycemia, Migraine, Seizure, and Postictal States
excerpt: Use history, glucose, exam evolution, and stroke scale scores carefully while still favoring stroke system activation when uncertainty remains high risk.
focus: fluctuating symptoms, gradual onset, and prior similar episodes as clues—not definitive exclusion alone
scenario: A patient has aphasia after a witnessed seizure with gradual improvement over 20 minutes.
examAngle: When large vessel occlusion cannot be excluded safely, transport and notification timelines still matter.

title: Crush Injury Syndrome: Rhabdomyolysis, Hyperkalemia, and EMS Field Considerations
excerpt: Anticipate compartment syndromes, renal threat, and lethal electrolyte shifts after prolonged extrication events.
focus: ECG changes from hyperkalemia, fluid strategy per protocol, and monitoring during extrication release
scenario: A patient was trapped under concrete for three hours with a crushed lower extremity and dark urine.
examAngle: Field recognition plus early notification and cardiac monitoring beats attempting definitive renal therapy in the field.

title: Snake Envenomation in EMS: Field Stabilization, Immobilization, and Transport Priorities
excerpt: Avoid harmful folklore interventions, immobilize the affected limb, monitor swelling progression, and document bite timing and species suspicion.
focus: coagulopathy risk, systemic symptoms, and airway swelling timelines
scenario: A hiker has progressive hand swelling and metallic taste after pit viper bite at a remote trailhead.
examAngle: Transport to antivenom-capable centers and airway vigilance outperform cutting or suction devices in modern teaching.

title: Altitude Illness: HAPE and HACE Recognition for EMS in Mountain Communities
excerpt: Differentiate benign acute mountain sickness from life-threatening cerebral and pulmonary edema patterns and prioritize descent plus oxygen.
focus: ataxia, altered mentation, frothy sputum, and hypoxia refractory to oxygen as escalation cues
scenario: A trekker at elevation has headache, vomiting, and confusion with ataxia after rapid ascent.
examAngle: Descent is definitive therapy in exam narratives; oxygen and transport support that priority.

title: Electrical Injury and Lightning Strike: Prehospital Assessment Patterns for EMS
excerpt: Address scene power mitigation, occult deep tissue injury, dysrhythmia surveillance, and trauma secondary to falls.
focus: entrance and exit wounds, compartment risk, and fixed pupils as misleading lightning sign per teaching
scenario: A lineman is found unconscious near a downed high-voltage line after a storm.
examAngle: Scene power safety is non-negotiable; exams reward staging until utility clearance when indicated.

title: Blast Injury Patterns for EMS: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Trauma Priorities
excerpt: Understand blast physiology, hidden internal injury risk, and triage priorities when structural collapse is involved.
focus: tympanic membrane injury as early marker teaching, pulmonary blast lung, and penetrating fragments
scenario: Patients present after an explosion at an industrial site with hearing loss, dyspnea, and shrapnel wounds.
examAngle: Primary blast lung injury can present without obvious external trauma; oxygenation and transport matter.

title: Geriatric Trauma and Ground-Level Falls: Anticoagulation, Head Injury Risk, and EMS Assessment
excerpt: Lower threshold for transport, include anticoagulant and antiplatelet history, and monitor for delayed intracranial hemorrhage education.
focus: occult shock, medication lists, and atypical presentation of serious injury
scenario: An 82-year-old on apixaban falls from standing with brief LOC and normal initial vitals.
examAngle: Low-mechanism geriatric trauma with head strike commonly leads to strong transport encouragement in exam keys.

title: Prehospital POCUS and EFAST: Conceptual Overview for Paramedic Learners
excerpt: Understand what EFAST adds when locally authorized, limitations without imaging follow-through, and how ultrasound complements physical exam.
focus: free fluid pockets, pneumothorax signs, and false negatives with rapid transport still required when unstable
scenario: An unstable hypotensive trauma patient may receive a limited FAST exam while resuscitation continues.
examAngle: Ultrasound never replaces hemorrhage control, airway management, or transport when the patient is crashing.

title: Field Hemorrhage Control: Tourniquets, Wound Packing, and Junctional Bleeding Strategies for EMS
excerpt: Prioritize external hemorrhage control before fluid debates, document tourniquet time, and integrate hemostatic gauze training with transport priorities.
focus: massive extremity bleeding, amputation scenes, and reassessment after packing
scenario: A construction worker has arterial spurting from the proximal thigh after a saw injury.
examAngle: Direct pressure and tourniquet application for extremity hemorrhage are frequent first-line correct answers.

title: Opioid Withdrawal in the Field: EMS Assessment, Safety, and Transport Considerations
excerpt: Differentiate withdrawal from sepsis, agitated delirium, and sympathomimetic toxidrome using vitals, history, and exam while prioritizing provider safety.
focus: autonomic hyperactivity, GI symptoms, pupillary findings as clusters, not single findings alone
scenario: A patient diaphoretic with dilated pupils is restless after missing methadone doses but denies chest pain.
examAngle: Supportive care, screening for other emergencies, and transport for definitive symptom control align with many protocols.

title: Suspected Malignant Hyperthermia Crisis: Field Recognition Concepts for EMS (Educational)
excerpt: Review educational recognition patterns tied to succinylcholine exposure in susceptible patients and the principle of rapid notification and supportive care.
focus: unexplained hyperthermia with muscle rigidity and rising EtCO2 in peri-arrest contexts per teaching scenarios
scenario: A postoperative transfer patient becomes rigid, febrile, and tachycardic with rising EtCO2 during transport.
examAngle: Rapid recognition, stopping triggering agents when applicable, aggressive cooling support, and urgent destination are common themes in educational items.
